q: Lipids are a group of substances that are highly soluble in water.
e: Lipids are a group of substances soluble in organic solvents and insoluble in water.

q: Lipids are soluble in organic solvents.
e: Lipids: are a group of substances soluble in organic solvents and insoluble in water.

q: Cholesterol is an essential component of cell membranes.
e: Cholesterol: Essential component of cell membranes.

q: Triglycerides are essential for the synthesis of bile acids.
e: Cholesterol is essential for bile acids synthesis.

q: Cholesterol acts as a precursor for the synthesis of steroid hormones.
e: Cholesterol is essential for synthesis of steroid hormon.

q: Triglycerides are primarily used as a structural component of the cell membrane.
e: Triglycerides are used as energy source in tissues and for energy storage in adipose tissue.

q: Adipose tissue primarily stores lipids in the form of triglycerides for energy.
e: Triglycerides: Used for energy storage in adipose tissue.

q: Phospholipids form bilayers that are a major component of cell membranes.
e: Phospholipids: Form phospholipid bilayers -a major component of the cell membrane.

q: Phospholipids do not participate in the transport of other lipids.
e: Phospholipids participate in the transport of other lipids.

q: A lipoprotein is a complex spherical structure designed to transport fats in plasma.
e: A lipoprotein: is a complex spherical structure... The lipoprotein system evolved to solve the problem of transporting fats in the aqueous environment of plasma.

q: The central core of a lipoprotein consists of polar components like free cholesterol.
e: A lipoprotein has a central core of hydrophobic lipids (triglycerides and cholesterol esters) and a surface layer of polar components (phospholipids, free cholesterol, proteins).

q: The surface layer of lipoproteins contains phospholipids, free cholesterol, and apolipoproteins.
e: surface layer of polar components (phospholipids, free cholesterol, proteins -the apolipoproteins).

q: Apolipoproteins can serve as activators or inhibitors of enzymes involved in lipoprotein metabolism.
e: Apolipoproteins have 4 Major Functions, including serving as activators or inhibitors of enzymes involved in the metabolism of lipoproteins.

q: Apolipoproteins do not act as ligands for lipoprotein receptors.
e: Apolipoproteins act as ligands for lipoprotein receptors.

q: Lipoproteins are defined by their density and vary in composition and function.
e: Lipoproteins are defined by their density and differ in composition, structure and function.

q: Chylomicrons are primarily produced by the liver after fasting.
e: Chylomicrons are produced by gut after a meal. Not present in normal fasting plasma.

q: The major apolipoprotein of chylomicrons is apoB-48.
e: Chylomicrons have major apolipoprotein apoB-48.

q: Very low density lipoprotein (VLDL) is the main carrier of dietary triglycerides.
e: VLDL is the main carrier of endogenously produced TGs. Chylomicrons are the main carrier of dietary TGs.

q: LDL is generated from VLDL in the circulation.
e: Low density lipoprotein (LDL) is generated from VLDL in the circulation.

q: Low density lipoprotein (LDL) is the main carrier of cholesterol from the liver to tissues.
e: LDL is the main carrier of cholesterol from liver to the tissues.

q: High density lipoprotein (HDL) has a major apolipoprotein called apoB-100.
e: HDL's major apolipoprotein is apoA-I. LDL and VLDL use apoB-100.

q: HDL performs a protective function by transporting excess cholesterol from tissues to the liver.
e: HDL has a protective function. Transport excess cholesterol from tissues to the liver for excretion.

q: Cholesterol can only be excreted from the body by way of the kidneys.
e: Cholesterol can only be excreted from the body by way of the liver.

q: Lipoprotein metabolism involves interconnected cycles that are centered on the liver.
e: Their metabolism can be thought two interconnected cycles centered on the liver.

q: A standard lipid profile test includes total cholesterol, HDL, LDL, and triglycerides.
e: Lipid Profile test includes: Total cholesterol, HDL-cholesterol, LDL-cholesterol, Triglyceride.

q: A lipid profile is used to help determine the risk of coronary heart disease (CHD).
e: Lipid profile is done to help determine risk of coronary heart disease (CHD).

q: LDL-cholesterol is commonly referred to as the 'Good cholesterol'.
e: HDL-cholesterol is (Good cholesterol), while LDL-cholesterol is (Bad cholesterol).

q: Cholesterol is a type of fat found in the blood and is classified as a steroid.
e: It is steroid. Cholesterol is a type of fat, found in the blood.

q: Approximately 70% of the body's cholesterol is derived directly from food.
e: 70% synthesized in body, 30% from food.

q: Dietary cholesterol comes exclusively from plant sources.
e: 30% from food (animal source as meat, eggs and dairy products).

q: High levels of blood cholesterol can lead to coronary artery disease.
e: Too much cholesterol leads to coronary artery disease.

q: Cholesterol is excreted from the body primarily in the form of bile salts.
e: Excretion: mainly excreted in the form of bile salts.

q: Cholesterol deficiency is beneficial and poses no negative effects on the body.
e: Therefore, cholesterol deficiency is not good. It is the precursor for many vital substances in our body.

q: Acetyl CoA is a precursor for the synthesis of cholesterol.
e: The chart shows Acetyl CoA pointing to Cholesterol.

q: Cholesterol is a direct precursor for the synthesis of Vitamin D and cortisol.
e: The chart shows Cholesterol acting as a precursor for Vitamin D, Cortisol, Bile salts, Aldosterone, Estrogen, Progesterone, and Testosterone.

q: A total cholesterol level of less than 200 mg/dl is considered desirable.
e: Total cholesterol <200 is Desirable.

q: A total cholesterol level of 220 mg/dl is classified as 'High'.
e: Total cholesterol between 200-239 is Borderline high.

q: A total cholesterol level of 250 mg/dl falls into the 'Borderline high' category.
e: Total cholesterol ≥240 is classified as High.

q: The standard wavelength used in the cholesterol test procedure is 520 nm.
e: Assay conditions: Wavelength: 520 nm (500-520).

q: During the cholesterol test, the instrument should be adjusted to zero using the standard solution.
e: Adjust the instrument to zero with distilled water.

q: The cholesterol test procedure requires pipetting 10 μl of the sample into a cuvette with 1 ml of reagent.
e: Pipette into a cuvette: R ml 1, Sample μl 10.

q: In the cholesterol test, the sample is incubated for 30 minutes at 37°C.
e: Mix and incubate for 5 min at 37°C or 10 min at 15-25°C.

q: The color developed in the cholesterol assay is stable for at least 60 minutes.
e: The color is stable for at least 60 minutes.

q: Familial hypercholesterolemia is an example of secondary hypercholesterolemia.
e: Primary hypercholesterolemia includes: Polygenic hypercholesterolemia, Familial hypercholesterolemia, Familial combined hyperlipidemia.

q: Hypothyroidism and nephrotic syndrome are causes of secondary hypercholesterolemia.
e: Secondary hypercholesterolemia: Nephrotic syndrome, Obstructive jaundice, Hypothyroidism.

q: Hyperthyroidism is clinically associated with hypercholesterolemia.
e: Hypocholesterolemia causes include hyperthyroidism.

q: Malabsorption and malnutrition can lead to hypocholesterolemia.
e: Hypocholesterolemia causes: Malabsorption and malnutrition.

q: Hypercholesterolemia is a major risk factor for the development of atherosclerosis.
e: Hypercholesterolemia is a major risk factor for Atherosclerosis.

q: A lipid profile should be assessed yearly in patients with a family history of premature CAD.
e: Therefore, it is every year to assess lipid profile in: ... A family history of premature CAD.

q: Patients whose plasma appears lipemic do not require a lipid profile assessment.
e: It is every year to assess lipid profile in: ... Patients whose plasma is seen to be lipemic.
